title: Back references in regular expressions should only refer to capturing groups that are matched before the reference
description: Ensures that back references in regular expressions do not refer to capturing groups that are defined after the reference, which would result in undefined or incorrect behavior.
recommendation: Review all regular expressions in the codebase and ensure that any back reference (e.g., \1, \2) only refers to a capturing group that appears before it in the pattern.
impact: Incorrect use of back references can lead to regular expressions that do not work as intended, potentially causing bugs or security issues.

noncompliant.code:
Pattern.compile("(a)\1(b)") // \1 refers to a group that is defined before, which is correct
Pattern.compile("(a)(b)\2") // \2 refers to group (b), which is defined before, which is correct
Pattern.compile("(a)\2(b)") // \2 refers to group (b), which is defined after, which is incorrect

compliant.code:
Pattern.compile("(a)(b)\1") // \1 refers to group (a), which is defined before, which is correct
Pattern.compile("(a)(b)\2") // \2 refers to group (b), which is defined before, which is correct
